entry.title: Re: SAP BTP-CPI-Package-Domain-Centric-Pattern (PDCP):Solving Package Sprawl at Scale-PART II
entry.content:
@vinaymittal thanks for the technical scrutiny. Let's look at the actual math of the DCRP/PDCP blueprint:

1. The 5-Minute Blitz & TCO
Velocity: Because I’ve mastered the logic, I recently deployed 4 proxies across 4 distinct domains using dynamic routing for 10 subprocesses each.
Scale: This allows receiving messages from 40 different vendors via only 4 proxies, fully configured in just 5 minutes vs maybe 15 or 20min each api-proxy adding policies, my solution provides stable template, plugin and play javascript in any APIM without extra coding, just need to follow the logic, KVM + JavaScript.
New vendors comming ? Change KVM string, takes 10 secunds, ready to use.
TCO: The framework delivers $280K in total value—$50K labor, $30K infra, and $200K in risk mitigation—by optimizing the SAP APIM licenses you already own.
2. Performance & Edge Cases
Validation: Stress-tested with 30,000+ messages, maintaining <100ms latency at a 99.92% success rate.
Hotfixes: iFlow DNA (7-segment naming) ensures individual iFlows remain granular and redeployable within consolidated PDCP packages.
Security: The DCRP Engine extracts "vendor" at runtime, allowing conditional PCI/Security policies only where needed, providing more control than traditional 1:1 proxies.
Established 1:1 patterns create "unmanageable graveyards of objects." This framework replaces manual labor with architectural intelligence.

Think that’s pushing it? Wait until Chapter 3: The Commander’s Blueprint to end chaos globally within SAP BTP Integration Suite.

Best regards,

Ricardo Viana Enterprise Integration Architect | The Commander

entry.title: Re: I want to confirm the value of LASTCHANGE_DATE in the...
entry.content:
Recommended approach would be

  1. Test in your ECC system: Check a sample of work centers, make changes, and confirm whether LASTCHANGE_DATETIME updates reliably.
  2. If consistent, you can safely use it for incremental extraction.
  3. If inconsistent, consider alternative fields like AEDAT (Last Change Date) or use CDHDR/CDPOS (Change Document tables) for guaranteed change tracking.

entry.title: Re: When flagging the pricing condition for deletion a se...
entry.content:
Why both records are flagged because

  • Condition records are identified by a combination of key fields (condition type, key combination, validity dates, etc.).
  • When you originally created the record with validity starting 01.01.2026 → 31.12.9999, then later adjusted it to start from 01.05.2026, SAP internally still treats the “infinite” record as part of the same condition record chain.
  • Because the system sees both records as belonging to the same logical condition (same condition type, same key, same price), the deletion flag propagates to the related record.
  • This is especially common when the condition type is a copy of PR00 (pricing condition), since PR00 logic enforces uniqueness of condition records per key combination.

Key Points to Check

Condition Record Key: Verify the key combination (customer/material, etc.) — if both records share the same key, SAP considers them part of the same condition.

Validity Interval Splitting: When you shortened the validity of the “infinite” record, SAP may have created overlapping or split records internally. Deletion can cascade across these linked records.

Deletion Flag Behavior: The flag is not just applied to the record you select; it applies to all records that SAP considers duplicates (same key + same condition value).

How to handle this is if you want to delete only one validity interval, you may need to adjust the validity dates rather than flagging for deletion. Alternatively, create a new condition record with a different key (e.g., different condition table or access sequence) to separate them. Check with transaction VK12 or VKM3 to see how SAP is grouping these records.

entry.title: SAP PO to SAP CI Migration - Simplifying File-Based Scenarios with SMB Adapter in SAP CI
entry.content:
Every enterprise today is actively working on SAP PO to SAP Cloud Integration (CI) migration as part of their move towards modern, cloud-based integration platforms. While many interface types can be migrated smoothly, one of the most common challenges during SAP PO to SAP Cloud Integration (CI) migration is handling file-based interfaces that rely on internal on-premise file systems. In many landscapes, business applications exchange data by placing files on shared network folders, and SAP PO integrations are tightly built around these locations.

In SAP PO, this was quite straightforward. The File/NFS Adapter allowed direct access to internal network paths without the need for FTP servers or external exposure. As a result, many legacy integrations continue to depend on this simple and secure approach.

But when moving to SAP Integration Suite, teams quickly realize an uncomfortable truth: SAP CI does not provide a native NFS adapter. Since SAP CI runs in the cloud, it cannot directly access internal file systems, making a direct “like-for-like” migration of such scenarios difficult.

This creates a big question:

How do we migrate scenarios where files are stored inside the customer’s internal network?

The Real-World Problem

  • Many SAP PO interfaces rely on reading and writing files from internal network drives.
  • These files are usually stored on centralized internal file servers.
  • Access to these locations happens strictly within the corporate network, not over the internet.

SAP PO and SAP CI Comparison:

AspectSAP POSAP CI
Access to Internal File SystemsNFS adapter could directly access internal network pathsNo native NFS adapter available
Additional InfrastructureNo need for FTP servers or external exposureCI runs on cloud which cannot directly access internal file systems
Security & SimplicitySecure and simple setup using internal mountsRequires alternative approach for on-prem file access
Migration ImpactFile-based scenarios worked seamlessly with NFSBlocks a direct “like-for-like” migration from SAP PO

 

Solution:

Option 1 – FTP/SFTP

A common workaround suggested in projects is to introduce an FTP/SFTP server and move files there first.

But highlight why this is not ideal:

  • FTP is not recommended for security reasons
  • Requires extra infrastructure

So we need something better.

Option 2 – SMB Adapter (Best fit)

SAP has released the SMB Adapter in SAP Integration Suite specifically designed to address this exact problem.

The SMB adapter facilitates file-based operations and accelerates implementation time to connect to a server using SMB (Server Message Block) protocol. The adapter is available only for Standard service plans and above of the SAP Integration Suite.

Feature highlights:

  • Secure authentication with Server Message Block (SMB) shares.
  • Offers compatibility with various SMB dialects.
  • On-Premise Connectivity via Cloud Connector.
  • Supports connections to SMB using Encryption, DFS, and Signing.
  • Provides different file handling options such as override, append, fail, and ignore.

 

High-Level File Based Architecture Explanation:

The above diagram illustrates the architectural shift when migrating file-based integrations from SAP PO to SAP Cloud Integration.

In SAP PO landscapes (Diagram 1), accessing files stored inside the customer’s internal network was relatively simple. The SAP PO File Adapter could directly read and write files from network locations using the NFS protocol.

Application servers typically mounted shared drives, and SAP PO accessed them seamlessly as local folders.

However, in SAP Cloud Integration ((Diagram 2) this approach is no longer possible. Since SAP CI runs in the cloud, it cannot directly mount or access internal network file systems. Moreover, SAP Integration Suite does not provide an NFS adapter like SAP PO.

This creates a major migration challenge for scenarios where:

  • Business applications drop files on internal network folders
  • SAP PO polls those folders using the File/NFS adapter
  • No FTP/SFTP servers are involved

To address this gap, SAP introduced the SMB Adapter in SAP Integration Suite.

The lower part of the diagram shows the modern CI-based approach:

  • Internal folders are exposed as SMB Shares on the customer network
  • SAP Cloud Connector establishes a secure tunnel between SAP CI and the on-premise network
  • The SMB Adapter in SAP CI connects through Cloud Connector
  • Communication happens over TCP using the SMB protocol
  • Files can be securely read from or written to internal locations without exposing them to the internet

This architecture provides a clean and secure replacement for NFS-based integrations in SAP PO, enabling a smooth migration path for file-based scenarios without introducing FTP servers or additional middleware.

Additional Scenario – When an SMB Server Already Exists

In many customer landscapes, an SMB file server is already available in the internal network (diagram 1), and applications directly place files on SMB shared folders. In SAP PO setups, even in such cases, the File/NFS adapter cannot consume these files directly. The SMB share first needs to be mounted on the SAP PO server and exposed as a local file path. Only after this mounting, SAP PO can access the location using the NFS/File adapter. This adds additional administrative effort and dependency on server configurations.

With SAP Cloud Integration, this limitation no longer exists. The SMB Adapter in SAP CI can connect directly to an existing SMB server through SAP Cloud Connector without any need for drive mounting. As long as the folder is available as an SMB share and properly exposed via Cloud Connector, SAP CI can immediately read from or write to it. This significantly simplifies the architecture, reduces infrastructure dependencies, and makes the migration of such scenarios from SAP PO to SAP CI much more straightforward.

In the Next Sections, We Will See:

  1. How to : Create or update an SMB Share on the network
  2. Configure: Cloud Connector for SMB access
  3. Build: Configure SMB Adapter in SAP CI
  4. Demo Scenario:
    • Pick a file from on-prem location
    • Process it in SAP CI
    • Delete the file as a part of post processing

Section A – Preparing the SMB Share

In our scenario, the internal file server is Linux-based. The following steps need to be executed by the Infrastructure team to create and expose a folder as an SMB share. These commands can be run on the Ubuntu server via SSH using tools like PuTTY or the command prompt.

# Update packages - before installing new components, ensure that the system packages are up to date
sudo apt update
 
# Install Samba and client tools required for SMB communication
sudo apt install samba smbclient -y
 
# Create the folder that will be exposed as an SMB share
sudo mkdir -p /srv/smb/smb_share
 
# Create SMB user
sudo adduser smbuser
 
# Set directory ownership and permissions
sudo chown -R smbuser:smbuser /srv/smb/smb_share
sudo chmod -R 775 /srv/smb/smb_share
 
# Backup Samba config
sudo cp /etc/samba/smb.conf /etc/samba/smb.conf.bak
 
# Configure the SMB Share - Add the new share definition to the Samba configuration
sudo bash -c 'cat >> /etc/samba/smb.conf <<EOF
 
[smb_share]
   path = /srv/smb/smb_share
   browseable = yes
   read only = no
   valid users = smbuser
   force user = smbuser
   create mask = 0660
   directory mask = 0770
EOF'
This configuration defines:
  • The physical path of the share
  • Read/write access
  • Authorized user
  • File and folder permission masks
# Create and enable the Samba-specific password for the SMB user:
sudo smbpasswd -a smbuser
sudo smbpasswd -e smbuser
 
# Restart Samba services
sudo systemctl restart smbd nmbd
 
# Allow Samba through firewall (if UFW is enabled)
sudo ufw allow samba
 
# Verify Samba configuration
testparm -s
 
# Verify share listing
smbclient -L localhost -U%
 
# Test share access
smbclient //localhost/smb_share -U smbuser

#Accessing the Share from Windows access: Once the SMB share is created, it can be accessed from any authorized Windows machine using the network path format using the credentials created above:
\\<server-name-or-ip>\smb_share

Deploy the credentials (smbuser) as basic credentials in SAP CI Security Artifact, these credentials will be configured in the SMB Adapter for authentication.

Section B – Cloud Connector Configuration

As we are connecting to On Premise Server, Cloud Connector comes into picture for the connectivity. Check out this article to setup TCP connection in Cloud Connector: https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/configure-access-control-tcp

 

Section C – Setup SMB Adapter in SAP CI Iflow

SAP CI IFlow:

 

 

Configuration of SMB Sender Adapter

 

Field

Description

Address

Specify the hostname or IP address of the SMB Server.

Specify Virtual Hostname if Cloud Connector is used

Domain

Specify the domain used for authentication.

Proxy Type

Specify the proxy type:

 • Internet • On-Premise

Location ID

Location ID of Cloud Connector (proxy type = On-Premise)

SMB Dialect

Select the SMB dialect or version to be used for communication.

An SMB dialect is a specific version of the SMB protocol that defines the supported features, capabilities, and communication formats between a client and a server

Share

Specify the SMB share folder which represents the network resource

Example: Assuming the mount path is \\ServerName\ShareName, you must specify Share as ShareName.

Authentication

Select the authentication type for connecting to the SMB server. Currently only Basic Authentication is supported

Credential Name

User Credentials security artifact

Timeout (in secs)

Specify the maximum waiting time (in seconds) for a response message from the SMB Server.

Enable Encryption

Enable to turn on encrypted communication between client & server.

This option is applicable only if the server supports it. This adds encryption to the data in transit, supported only for SMB 3.x dialect

Enable DFS

Enable Distributed File System

DFS in SMB is a feature that provides a unified virtual file system namespace, allowing multiple SMB file shares across different servers to be accessed through a single logical path.

Enable Signing

Enable SMB Signing.

This option is applicable only if the server supports it. This adds signing to the data in transit. supported only for SMB 3.x dialect

Maximum Reconnect Attempts

Specify the maximum number of retries permissible for reconnection to the SMB server before message processing starts.

The processing tab contains the following fields:

This tab has parameters similar to what we see in SFTP Adapter Processing.

Please check this document for detailed information: https://api.sap.com/odata/1.0/catalog.svc/Files('2ee08d2557d84acfab3331448c2784b1')/$value

 

Section D - Demo:

In the Demo, the idea is to read a file from the SMB Share using SMB Sender Adapter.

SMB Share:

 

 

 

 

Content in the File:

The Sender Adapter Configuration is shown as above, Lets deploy the IFlow now.

Message run in TRACE mode:

File is received in SAP CI and deleted from the SMB Share.

In this similar manner, you can also use SMB receiver adapter to write a file on the folder, the connection tab configuration remains same as Sender Adapter, Operation Type in the Processing tab will be changed to “Create File”

Conclusion:

Migrating file-based scenarios from SAP PO to SAP CI no longer needs to be a roadblock.

The SMB Adapter provides a clean, secure, and SAP-recommended approach to connect SAP Integration Suite with internal network file systems making it the closest and most reliable replacement for the NFS adapter of SAP PO.

With minimal changes to existing infrastructure and no need for FTP servers, the SMB adapter truly simplifies one of the trickiest parts of PO to CI migration.

 

Hope this help,

Cheers!

Punith

entry.title: Re: Recommendation for Standard API / Function Module – Q...
entry.content:
Hi,

thank you for the details provided.

1.)  The referred function module - QB_VB_QINF_POST - is a non-released one, which means it is supported during SAP internal use cases only. If you plan to use it in a custom development, that can be done for own responsibility. For more information, see KBA:

1909989 - FAQ - Function Modules usage and status.

2.) A released module (BAPI) is not provided for quality information records (QINF).

3.) As of SAP S/4HANA 2020, there is an API available - API_QUALITYINFORECORD_SRV - to update QINF records, see the details below:

///API_Explorer///
'Quality Info Record'
https://api.sap.com/api/OP_API_QUALITYINFORECORD_SRV_0001/resource/Quality_Info_Record_Data?ReleaseInfo=2020%20FPS02

///SAPHelp///
'Quality Info Record'
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/a08e12a754cf4891b41a01a285d065bb/e568441aef594a4dba2f98ca53f4c85f.html

Have a nice day,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Create Subcontracting process
entry.content:
Why the error occurs is because

Recursive BOM: When the header and component are identical, SAP interprets this as a circular dependency. Subcontracting logic doesn’t allow the same material to be both supplied and consumed.

Serial Number Status: After the 541 movement, the serial number is in ESTO (stock at vendor). At GR, the system expects consumption of components and return of the repaired material. Because the component is the same as the header, the system cannot resolve the serial number status correctly.

Options to Handle Repair Subcontracting

1. Use a Dummy Component Approach

  • Create a “repair service” or dummy material as the component in the BOM.
  • This allows the subcontracting PO to be created without recursion.
  • The dummy component is consumed, while the serialized header material is issued (541) and received back (101).

2. Service PO Instead of Subcontracting PO

  • If no physical components are consumed, consider using a service PO for repair charges.
  • The serialized material can be sent to vendor with movement 541Q (stock provided to vendor) and received back with movement 101Q.
  • The service PO covers the repair cost, while the material movement handles the logistics.

3. Custom Process / Enhancements

  • Explore implementing user exits or BAdIs to bypass the BOM requirement for repair scenarios.
  • This avoids recursive BOMs and allows direct subcontracting of serialized items.

entry.title: Re: Project stock and WBS element status CLOSED
entry.content:
Why you can close a WBS with Stock or Open Commitments is because of the following:

System Status vs. Business Rules:
The system status CLSD (Closed) on a WBS element only prevents new postings (e.g., no new actual costs, commitments, or stock movements). It does not check whether existing stock, open POs/PRs, or balances are zero.

Settlement Profile Not Relevant Here:
Settlement profile controls whether costs must be settled before technically completing or closing. Since your WBS has no settlement profile, there’s no system check for balances.

Valuated Project Stock:
Even with valuated project stock, SAP does not enforce “stock must be zero” before closing. The stock remains in MBBS, but you cannot issue or receive against the closed WBS anymore.

Open POs/PRs:
The system does not automatically block closing if commitments exist. Instead, once the WBS is closed, you cannot create new commitments or post goods receipts/invoices against it. The existing commitments remain in reporting until they are manually closed or deleted.

How SAP expects you to handle this

  • System Status CLSD is a technical lock, not a reconciliation check.
  • If your business requires “no stock, no open commitments” before closure, you need to enforce this via:
    • User Status profiles with checks (e.g., status check functions in OPSI).
    • Custom validations in user exits (e.g., CJ20N exit) to prevent closure if stock or commitments exist.
    • Process discipline: run MBBS/commitment reports before closing and clear balances.

entry.title: Re: My experience with Joule for Consultants PoC
entry.content:
Thanks for testing for the rest of us 👍 Conclusion: it has potential, but it's not quite there yet.

Which worries me, since examples of for example using Claude Code for code generation simply blows people away. Of course SAP and ABAP is a niche market and so far a pretty walled garden, but it cannot sustain itself if in the world around it development is faster and cheaper.

Combine that with the experience that even Joule mixes solutions for different SAP versions (on-prem, private and public) into an answer... not sparking joy at the moment.

Which leads me to the question: In its current state, does it really save you a lot of time? Your example of analyzing existing code is a good one, in that area I can see how Joule can be of value. What other use-cases does it speed up?

In a world where AI seems to be taking over everything, expectations are high. So far, we're falling behind. We seriously need Joule to mature fast and catch up to become our go-to assistant.

Or am I seeing things a little too bleak? In that case, please cheer me up 😄

entry.title: Net due date in down payment requests posted from SD
entry.content:
It is noticed that for down payment requests, the net due date in the posting document is always the document date. The expectation is that the net due date to be calculated based on the payment terms.

Apparently, the payment terms for the invoice or from the customer master data is also not transferred to the down payment request.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Net due date in down payment requests posted from SD
entry.content:
This is the standard behavior. SD module deletes the contents of the following fields in customer line items before passing them to accounting/FI for down payment requests:

  • ZTERM (terms of payment key)
  • ZBD1T (cash discount days 1)
  • ZBD2T (cash discount days 2)
  • ZBD3T (cash discount days 3)
  • ZBD1P (cash discount percentage 1)
  • ZBD2P (cash discount percentage 2)

Only the baseline date for payment (ZFBDT) is transferred to FI. In general, this is the billing date.

entry.title: SAP ANALYTICS CLOUD - Hierarchy Update Issue - New members not added in <root>
entry.content:
Hello,

I am facing the following case:

  • Public Dimension with Hierarchy, initially defined with a file. 
  • Hierarchy updated occasionally through file.
  • Leaf members updated daily.
    • On this import job there is no field matched to Hierarchy since it would override the parents already added through the file.
  • New parent members do not exist yet, since they are updated from a file. New base members are loaded with empty hierarchy (not root).
  • Other master data updates on the same dimension fail, since there are members without any value in the hierarchy field.

The target is to add new Public Dimension leaf members in <root> of the existing hierarchy without affecting the hierarchy members that already exist (since they are updated manually from a file source). This will prevent error on the daily master data updates and allow fact data of these members to present in stories without daily adding these new members in the root of the hierarchy. 

The dimension setting "Group and show under Not in Hierarchy" does not work since there is no mapping on Hierarchy field during import.

The option to load everything on <root> and then reimport manually (daily) the file with the known hierarchy info is not an approach that we want to use at the moment.

Is there an approach you would suggest me to try?

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Automatic FOC indicator should be marked if Service o...
entry.content:
In S/4HANA Service, the requirement you describe — automatically creating a FOC (Free of Charge) purchase order when the external system flags a part/service as “under vendor warranty” — can be handled, but not directly through standard warranty claim functionality (since you don’t have warranty master data in SAP). Instead, you need to leverage account assignment and PO control logic.

Why Standard Warranty claim doesn’t apply because SAP’s warranty claim functionality requires warranty master data (validity periods, objects, etc.), which you don’t have. Without that, the system cannot automatically determine warranty eligibility.

Options in S/4HANA Service

1. External Flag → PO Control
The external system sends an indicator (e.g., “Warranty = Yes”) along with the service order. This indicator can be mapped to a custom field or account assignment category in the service order. During PO creation, you use this flag to determine the FOC indicator (e.g., “Free of Charge” item category or special stock indicator).

2. Condition Technique / Pricing
You can configure a pricing condition or accounting indicator that sets the net value of the PO item to zero when the warranty flag is present. This ensures the PO is created with FOC status, even though the vendor is supplying the part/service.

3. Item Category Customization
Define a special item category for warranty items. When the external system passes the warranty flag, the PO item is created with this item category, which automatically sets the FOC indicator.

4. BAdI / Enhancement
If you want full automation, you can implement a BAdI in MM-PUR (e.g., ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST) to check the warranty flag and set the FOC indicator programmatically during PO creation.

entry.title: Helpful tips and tricks to save time when developing in SAP Data Services - Part 1
entry.content:
Did you ever have to deal with a wide table or an input file format containing dozens—or even hundreds—of columns, each storing different attributes of a single record?

And did you then find yourself cleaning those fields, applying functions, adjusting data types, and mapping everything to a target structure one column at a time?

If so, you’re definitely not alone. Fortunately, SAP Data Services provides a built-in feature that can dramatically reduce the effort required for exactly these kinds of scenarios.

The Challenge: Wide Files and Manual Effort

Let’s assume you are tasked with processing large and wide input files—such as .txt, .csv, or even .xlsx—using SAP Data Services.
Your goal is to load this data into a database in a clean, uniform, and standardized format, so it can be processed further downstream.

Manually applying cleansing logic and data type adjustments to each column quickly becomes inefficient and error-prone when dealing with large schemas.

The Solution: Bulk Column Adjustments in a Query Transform

The SAP Data Services Designer Client includes a built-in solution for bulk modifications of multiple attributes at once.

To make use of it, insert a Query transform directly after your file or table input and pull all input attributes into the output schema.

Now you can select the columns you want to adjust:

  • Click the first attribute, scroll down, and Shift-click the last one

  • Or simply use Ctrl + A to select all output columns

  • If you only want to work on a subset, use Ctrl-click to select individual attributes

Once the desired columns are selected, right-click on any highlighted column and choose “Properties…”:

Multiple Column Properties Window

You are now in the Multiple Column Properties window. All previously selected attributes are grouped together, allowing you to apply changes to all of them in one go.

In this example, let’s assume we want to:

  • Change the data type of all selected columns to VARCHAR(80)

  • Apply a basic cleansing logic to every field

To do this:

  • Enable the Data type checkbox and set the length to 80

  • Disable Content type and Description

  • Keep the Mapping checkbox enabled

Applying Identical Logic Using Macro Substitutions

In the Mapping section, you can apply identical transformation logic to all selected columns while keeping their original names intact.

This is achieved using the {original_expression} macro.

Example mapping logic:

ltrim(rtrim(upper(nvl(FF_PCO_EMS_EXTRACT.ExpectedClosingDate,'')),' '), ' ')
 
This expression:

  • Trims leading and trailing spaces

  • Converts values to uppercase

  • Replaces NULL values using NVL (also known as COALESCE)

Once you click OK, the mapping logic and data type changes are applied to all selected output columns at once.

The Result

Voilà!
You have just mass-updated data types and mapping logic for a large number of fields in seconds.

This approach is especially useful when:

  • Working with very wide files or tables

  • Applying standard cleansing rules early in a dataflow

  • Grouping columns into subsets that require different treatments (e.g. different data types or functions)

If you have ever relied on copy & paste or manual adjustments for each attribute in the Designer Client, this technique will save you a significant amount of time—and frustration.

Give it a try. I hope it helps anyone out there who is struggling with wide tables or file formats!

Julian

entry.title: Invoice Creation BAPI from Purchase Order
entry.content:
Hi all.

I am trying to replicate the behaviour from MIRO transaction using BAPI_INCOMING_INVOICE_CREATE1, in order to create an invoice based on a purchase order.

In MIRO, I only need to enter the PO number, and the system will automatically retrieve the PO items with the pending amount. Besides, in scenarios where a PO item has 2 or more good receipt documents, the PO item is splitted in N invoice lines, each one with reference to the PO item and its material document.

However, when using the BAPI, I cannot just pass the PO number because it's asking a lot of mandatory fields, such as quantity, amount, tax code, etc. I am having a bit of trouble to apply the same logic as in MIRO, in order to build the invoice items in the same way, especially in scenarios with partial goods receipts, partial invoiced items, etc.

I am not able to find any FM or class used by the MIRO transaction to reuse it.  Is there any standard way to do this? 

I find it very strange that, in order to create an invoice based on an purchase order, I have to implement all the logic of obtaining the PO history, calculating quantities and pending amounts, considering cancellations, etc.

entry.title: What's new in SAP Cloud Application Programming Model – January 2026
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

We are excited to introduce our December 2025 and January 2026 releases of the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP). Our highlights include the new @assert feature, our revised CAP documentation, several enhancements to the CDS editor, and new features for deploying your CAP applications to the SAP BTP, Kyma Runtime.

Whether you are just getting started with CAP or are an experienced CAP developer, these updates aim to make your development experience smoother and more efficient. Stay tuned for more updates and mark your calendars for our upcoming events, including the first CAP Customer Roundtable of 2026 and Code Connect 2026.

 

Simplified Assertions with the New @assert Annotation

Declarative programming focuses on describing the intent rather than describing how to achieve it. This reduces the need for custom code, ensures consistency, and facilitates the expression of business logic. CAP offers numerous features supporting declarative programming. For a detailed overview read the following blog post: Declarative Programming in CAP: Less Code, More Value.

The December 2025 release of CAP introduces a new declarative feature that drastically reduces the amount of custom code needed in many applications. With the @assert annotation, you can now define more complex validation logic within your CDS definitions, eliminating the need for additional code. The @assert annotation supports the 'case when' syntax, allowing multiple validations to be executed sequentially. Furthermore, the returned error messages can be customized to be either a static string or a message key for internationalization (i18n) support. This enhancement helps to maintain data integrity and to provide clear and early feedback to users (for example by editing data in a draft) where validation rules are violated.

Read our documentation to learn all the details about the new @assert feature.

 Screenshot: The new @assert feature

 

Revised CAP Documentation: Finding Your Way Just Got Easier

In our latest release, we've refined and streamlined our CAP documentation to make it easier to find relevant information quickly.  We reorganized the docs into Get Started, Develop, and Deploy, so you can jump straight to the phase you’re in. Many guides were refreshed to match current CAP features and best practices, and new ones were added where we saw gaps.  Here are some key changes and additions you'll find:

Get Started Section

The initial guides received substantial updates. In a Nutshell now emphasizes staying current with CAP releases while streamlining setup instructions. The Bookshop Sample has been rewritten to align with current best practices. The Learn More section highlights most recommended learning resources, including Featured Samples and the qmacro series, making it clearer where to invest your learning time. Get Help includes an overview of main support channels and an improved troubleshooting guide.

Database Guides

Three new guides have been added addressing the following topics: CAP-Level Database Support, CDL Compiled to DDL, and Adding Initial Data.

Integration Section

This is an entirely new section. We've consolidated guidance on service integration, data federation, and inner-loop development. If you've been using the old Consuming (Remote) Services guide, you'll find its successor here with updated approaches.

Security & Data Privacy

This section has been restructured completely with new chapters on Authentication focusing on IAS, assigned user claims like AMS-Policies, and Outbound Authentication.

These updates aim to make your experience with our CAP documentation smoother and more efficient. We hope you find these enhancements useful, and as always, your feedback is invaluable to us.  

 

CDS Editor Updates

Our January 2026 release brings several enhancements to the CDS editor, designed to further enhance your development experience. Here’s what’s new:

  • Multi-Cursor Support for Selection Ranges: Selection ranges now also work with VS Code's multi-cursor mode, enabling you to edit multiple lines simultaneously.

 Screenshot: Multi-cursor support

  • Folding Ranges Improvements: Consecutive using statements are now treated as a single collapsible block, reducing clutter when folding code sections.

 Screenshot: Expanded using statement

 Screenshot: Collapsed using statement

  • Faster References Search and Workspace Symbols (Beta): Find References and Workspace Symbols operations now utilize a persisted index, which can significantly improve performance in larger projects with multiple independent CDS files.
    To enable this beta feature, set cds.workspace.persistency.enabled in the settings of your IDE. We're interested in your feedback on performance improvements or any issues you encounter.
  • Organized User Settings: Navigating user settings is now simpler with category grouping. The editor organizes settings into categories, making it easier to find and configure the settings you need.

 Screenshot: User settings organized in categories

 We hope these enhancements will make your development process smoother and more efficient. We welcome your feedback and suggestions as we continue to improve the CDS editor.

 

New features for Kyma Deployment

Our January release brings several new features for deploying your CAP applications to the SAP BTP, Kyma Runtime. For multitenant Kyma applications, we’ve introduced the new mtx-upgrade task, which is automatically added as a Kubernetes job by cds add multitenancy. This feature simplifies consistent upgrades across multiple tenants.

Another new addition is cds debug for Kubernetes. This command enables you to open a Chrome Node.js debugging session for your remote application running in Kyma. To see the available applications, use kubectl get deployments.

Example: run cds debug bookshop-srv --k8s to start a debugging session.

 Screenshot: New features for Kyma Deployment

These additions streamline both the deployment workflow for multitenant scenarios and the debugging process for applications running in Kyma.

 

Expert Session: Getting Started with CAP Node.js

Join us for an insightful session with expert Daniel Schlachter, in conversation with DJ Adams, as they guide you through embarking on your CAP Node.js project journey. This session covers essential topics such as initialization, data modelling, the cds command line tool, service creation, data mocking, annotations, event handlers, and declarative definitions. You will gain practical insights into best practices for CAP development that will help you structure your CAP projects efficiently from the start.

Don't miss this opportunity to learn from the experts and elevate your development practices.

 

Events

Next customer roundtable

The first CAP Customer Roundtable in 2026 is coming up soon! We invite you to join us on February 18th, 2026, at 5.00 PM CET. As always, we will provide you an overview of some of the latest features, insightful demos, and discussions on questions from the CAP community. Please refer to this blog post for additional details, including the agenda and dial-in information.

 

Mark your Calendars for Code Connect 2026

Code Connect returns for its third edition from July 13-16, 2026, in St. Leon-Rot, Germany. Online participation is also possible. The conference brings together UI5con, reCAP, and HANA Tech Con, offering a comprehensive program for SAP developers. Whether you are experienced or new to SAP technologies, Code Connect provides an opportunity to engage with peers, share knowledge, and explore the latest in SAP development.

The call for proposals is now open! We invite you to submit your own ideas and share your expertise with the community. Don’t wait and submit your session proposal by 13 March 2026 at the latest.

  • Call for proposals UI5con
  • Call for proposals reCAP
  • Call for proposals HANA Tech Con

Additionally, we are seeking sponsors to help make this event even better. Read our sponsorship prospectus to check our sponsorship opportunities. If you or your organization are interested in supporting Code Connect 2026, we'd love to hear from you.

Find all the details in this blog post and make sure to mark your calendars for this great event!

 

For further information on new features, please have a look at the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model Release Notes. If you want to stay up-to-date with news, learning resources, and product and strategy updates for CAP: follow our tag in the SAP Community.

We look forward to seeing how you use these new capabilities to create innovative solutions and drive your projects forward. Stay tuned for more updates and happy coding! 😊

entry.title: Issue with Performance Rating in Compensation
entry.content:
Dear all,

My customer is using the integration between Performance Management and Compensation.

In the performance form, we have:

  • The Summary section, which contains the Overall Manual Rating
  • The Performance–Potential Summary section, which contains separate Performance and Potential ratings

This year, the Overall Performance rating scale was changed to a 4-point scale. However, the Performance rating in the Performance–Potential Summary was not updated and is still using the old 3-point scale from last year.

We only discovered this during Compensation testing. And all pm-forms are already in the final step before completion. 

In the Compensation form, we selected the default rating and configured it to use the 4-point rating scale. As a result, the system now:

  • Pulls the Performance rating from the Performance–Potential Summary (3-point scale)
  • Displays it using the labels of the 4-point scale

This leads to incorrect and misleading ratings in Compensation. My assumption is that this is a configuration issue in the performance form. In my understanding, the Overall Performance rating and the Overall Manual Score should refer to the same final performance result.

Is there a way to configure the system so that Compensation uses the Overall Manual Score entered by the manager instead of the Performance rating from the Performance–Potential Summary?

Kind Regards,

Anne

entry.title: Будуй майбутнє з SAP: стажування в команді Sales
entry.content:
Ти ще встигаєш, стажування в SAP Ukraine чекає на тебе!

Шукаємо амбітного студента, який готовий підкорювати світ Sales разом із лідером ринку. Це не просто стажування — це реальний досвід у міжнародному середовищі.

Що на тебе чекає?

  • Участь у бізнес-дослідженнях і аналізі ринку.

  • Допомога у створенні маркетингових кампаній та розвитку локальної SAP Community.

  • Підтримка команди Sales під час зустрічей із клієнтами та партнерами.

  • Розвиток власної мережі контактів і навичок у глобальному середовищі.

Ти наш ідеальний кандидат, якщо:

✅Навчаєшся на 4–5 курсі (маркетинг, менеджмент, IT, фінанси).

✅Вільно розмовляєш українською та англійською.

✅Прагнеш професійного драйву.

Чому саме SAP?
SAP — це глобальна технологічна компанія, що об’єднує понад 100 000 фахівців у всьому світі та підтримує 400 000 клієнтів у різних галузях. Приєднуйся, щоб отримати досвід у маркетингу, аналітиці та продажах світового рівня!

Локація: Україна
Статус студента — обов’язковий

Твій старт у SAP вже тут 👉 подати заявку

entry.title: Focused Build: SP16 - My Test Executions Feature Of Showing error/warning message for open defects
entry.content:
This feature is introduced in ST-OST SP16 to ensure testers do not set test cases mistakenly to successfully tested, customers wanted to define some rules to guide the testers.

When you are trying set any status in colour green (like OK, OK with Reservation, Retest OK) this setting will be checking if the defect list of the tester meeting the threshold limit.

Then we get error or warning message based on the threshold setting.

You can maintain entries on priority values base and error/warning with threshold numbers.

 

In warning message, users are allowed to proceed by skipping this message. 

Let's discuss on various cases we can achieve from this setting:

1. With all priorities maintained

2. Missing priorities in config

3. Making default warning/error:  by this setting all testers will get message if any defects are there.

Also application should be refreshed once the configuration is updated in order to reflect the changes. This check will be performed for each steps for defects assigned to the steps and total defetcs and test case defects are considered with final status.

Please note: Always error type wins , if it meets the threshold.

entry.title: Vibe Coding my 1st SAP Digital Manufacturing POD Plugin with AI
entry.content:
How I built a fully functional SAP Digital Manufacturing POD extension in under an hour using Claude, Cline, and the power of AI-assisted development.

🚀The Challenge

I wanted to add some quick filters and sorting functionality to the standard Worklist POD Plugin in SAP Digital Manufacturing. The standard Worklist shows all SFCs (Shop Floor Controls) in a list, but operators frequently need to filter by status (New, Active, On Hold, etc.) and sort by different fields.

🛠️ The Tech Stack

Here's the AI-powered development stack I used:

  • VS Code: IDE and development environment.
  • Cline: AI coding assistant extension for VS Code.
  • SAP AI Core: Enterprise AI infrastructure on SAP BTP, providing my secure access to a LLM.
  • Anthropic Claude 4.5 Opus: Large Language Model powering the intelligence.

🎬The Development Session

Step 1: Context Gathering

I started by pointing Claude at SAP's official extension samples repository:

"Check all the code in the digital-manufacturing-extension-samples folder, so you learn how to create SAP Digital Manufacturing plugins."

Within seconds, Claude had analyzed:
- 30+ extension provider examples
- Core plugin extension patterns
- The components.json registration format
- Extension lifecycle and event hooks

Step 2: Architecture Decision

I described my goal:

"Create a Worklist Filter & Sort plugin that adds quick filter buttons to filter SFCs by status and a sort dropdown to the Worklist toolbar."

Claude immediately understood this should be a Core Plugin Extension (not a View Plugin), because:
- No separate UI component needed
- Extends existing Worklist toolbar
- Uses the `createToolbar` extension point

Step 3: Iterative Development

This is where vibe coding shines. The development was a conversation:

**Me:** "The extension is uploaded but not loading"

**Claude:** *Analyzes the error, identifies the wrong import path, fixes it*

**Me:** "Now I get a 400 error when filtering"

**Claude:** *Realizes the OData service doesn't support server-side filtering, refactors to client-side visibility filtering*

**Me:** "IT WORKS! 🎉"

Step 4: Production-Ready Code

Claude didn't just write working code, it created:
- Proper error handling
- Debug logging
- Clear code documentation
- A comprehensive README

📊The Results

Development Time: ~45 minutes
Lines of Code: ~600
Files Created: 12
Iterations to Working Solution: 8
Coffee Consumed: 1 cup ☕

🔑Key Learnings

1. AI Excels at Pattern Matching
Claude learned the SAP extension patterns from the samples and applied them consistently. When something didn't work, it could quickly identify what was different from the working examples.

2. Error Messages Are Gold
Every error message I shared helped Claude narrow down the issue. The AI doesn't just guess, it uses the error context to make informed fixes.

3. Human + AI = Superpowers
I knew what I wanted to build and why.
Claude knew how to implement it.
Together, we built something neither could have done as quickly alone.

4. Vibe Coding Requires Trust
You have to trust the process.
Sometimes the AI takes a different approach than you expected, but if you explain your constraints, it adapts.

🔗Get the Code

The complete extension is available on GitHub:

👉SAP Digital Manufacturing - Worklist Filter & Sort Extension

To use it:
1. Clone the repository
2. Update `designer/components.json` with your POD name and plant
3. Create a ZIP file
4. Upload to SAP DM with namespace: `sap.dm.custom.plugin.worklistfilterandsort`

Happy vibe coding! 🎵💻

entry.title: Re: Create SAML2.0 External Request in ABAP
entry.content:
I was able to build the SAML2 XML.  First of all, I defined a custom structure for the eIDAS attributes.

DATA(saml2_extensions) = VALUE xsdany( ).
DATA(eidas_attributes) = VALUE zsaml2p_eidas_attribute_t( (
  friendlyname = `RelayState`
  name         = `http://es.minhafp.clave/RelayState`
  nameformat   = `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:attrname-format:uri`
  isrequired   = abap_false
  value        = VALUE #( ( get_secure_random( i_size = 8 ) ) ) ) ).

CALL TRANSFORMATION zsaml2_eidas_attribute SOURCE saml2_eidas_attributes = eidas_attributes RESULT XML saml2_extensions.

I needed to create a simple SAP transformation to convert the ABAP structure to XML.

<?sap.transform simple?>
<tt:transform xmlns:tt="http://www.sap.com/transformation-templates"
              xmlns:samlp="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:protocol"
              xmlns:eidas="http://eidas.europa.eu/saml-extensions"
              xmlns:eidasnp="http://eidas.europa.eu/attributes/naturalperson"
              xmlns:ddic="http://www.sap.com/abapxml/types/dictionary"
              xmlns:def="http://www.sap.com/abapxml/types/defined">

  <tt:root name="SAML2_EIDAS_ATTRIBUTES" type="ddic:ZSAML2P_EIDAS_ATTRIBUTE_T"/>

  <tt:template>
    <samlp:Extensions>
      <eidas:RequestedAttributes>
        <tt:loop ref=".SAML2_EIDAS_ATTRIBUTES">
          <eidas:RequestedAttribute>
            <tt:attribute name="Name" value-ref="NAME"/>
            <tt:cond check="not-initial(NAMEFORMAT)">
              <tt:attribute name="NameFormat" value-ref="NAMEFORMAT"/>
            </tt:cond>
            <tt:cond check="not-initial(FRIENDLYNAME)">
              <tt:attribute name="FriendlyName" value-ref="FRIENDLYNAME"/>
            </tt:cond>
            <tt:cond check="not-initial(ISREQUIRED)">
              <tt:attribute name="isRequired" value-ref="ISREQUIRED"/>
            </tt:cond>
            <tt:group>
              <tt:cond frq="?">
                <tt:loop ref="VALUE">
                  <eidas:AttributeValue>
                    <tt:value ref="$ref"/>
                  </eidas:AttributeValue>
                </tt:loop>
              </tt:cond>
            </tt:group>
          </eidas:RequestedAttribute>
        </tt:loop>
      </eidas:RequestedAttributes>
    </samlp:Extensions>
  </tt:template>
</tt:transform>

In order to generate the SAML2 ticket, I used a standard ABAP structure.

DATA(issue_instant) = VALUE saml2_datetime( ).
GET TIME STAMP FIELD issue_instant.

DATA(saml_request) = VALUE saml2p_authn_request(
  request = VALUE #(
    id            = get_secure_random( i_size = 64 )
    version       = if_saml20_constants=>co_saml_version
    issueinstant  = issue_instant
    destination   = `https://se-pasarela.clave.gob.es/Proxy2/ServiceProvider`
      consent       = `urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:consent:unspecified`
      extensions    = saml2_extensions )
    nameid       = VALUE #( allowcreate = abap_true idformat = wssec_co_saml_nid_form_unspec )
    authncontext = VALUE #( comparison = if_saml20_constants=>co_comparison_method_s_minimum authncontextclassref = VALUE #( ( `http://eidas.europa.eu/LoA/low` ) ) )
    forceauthn   = abap_true
    ispassive    = abap_false
    providername = `S2833002E_E04975701;Demo-SP`
    assertionconsumerserviceurl = `https://pre-pasarela.clave.gob.es/SP2/ReturnPage` ).

 The next step is the signature, using standard classes. The important part here is to set the SAML2 Request Schema in the signature transformation.

TRY.
    DATA(xml_signer) = cl_sec_sxml_dsignature=>create_writer_instance( ).

    " Digestion over ID attritube of samlp:AuthnRequest node
    xml_signer->set_attributes(
      if_str_transform       = abap_false   " Disable standard transformation
      if_attribute_name      = 'ID'
      if_attribute_namespace = if_saml20_constants=>co_saml_protocol_support ).

    " ENV-C14N-EXC signature with RSA-SHA512 algorithm
    xml_signer->m_pse_context              = cl_sec_sxml_dsignature=>co_ssfa.
    xml_signer->m_signature_type           = cl_sec_sxml_dsignature=>co_type_enveloped.
    xml_signer->m_signature_transformation = cl_sec_sxml_dsignature=>co_transform_envelope.
    xml_signer->m_canonicalization         = cl_sec_sxml_dsignature=>co_c14n_exclusive.
    xml_signer->m_signature_ns_prefix      = cl_sec_sxml_dsignature=>co_signature_nsprefix.
    xml_signer->m_signature_id             = space.
    xml_signer->m_dsig_hash_algorithm      = 'SHA512'.
    xml_signer->m_ssf_hash_algorithm       = 'SHA512'.
    xml_signer->m_dsig_method              = cl_sec_sxml_dsignature=>co_dsig_method_rsa.

    " Use SAML2 schema for the XSLT transformation
    xml_signer->set_transformation(
      EXPORTING
        if_name           = 'SAML2_AUTHN_REQUEST'
        if_data_tab       = VALUE #( ( name = 'SAML2_AUTHN_REQUEST' value = saml_request ) )
      CHANGING
        ch_signature_data = saml_request->request-signature_raw ).

    " Sign using PSE certificate stored in STRUST
    xml_signer->sign_xml(
      if_ssf_app      = if_saml20_constants=>co_ssfappl_sp_sign  " S2SVPS
      if_add_keyinfo  = abap_true
      if_re_transform = abap_true ).
  CATCH cx_sec_sxml_error.
    RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_saml20_core EXPORTING textid = cx_saml20_core=>xml_create_error.
ENDTRY.

 I used an already existing STRUST app to store the certificate for the signature. However, you can use a custom one if you want.

Once the XML is signed, you can generate the final XML using a standard transformation.

CALL TRANSFORMATION saml2_authn_request SOURCE saml2_authn_request = saml_request RESULT XML DATA(signed_xml) OPTIONS xml_header = 'no'.

With this approach, I was able to generate the SAML2 ticket with the required format.

entry.title: SAP Ariba is now integrated with Microsoft Sentinel Solution for SAP
entry.content:
Quick link to GitHub.

Supply chain is a critical topic in almost every industry these days. We live in times where a controversial social media post and actions of government officials can disrupt factory operations almost the next day. See this Reuters (2025) article that sheds light on car production halt in Germany caught in the crossfire of political turmoil in 2 other countries. SAP Ariba helps diversify the risk between buyers and suppliers in tightly interconnected supply chains.

What a juicy target for cyber criminals one might say 😉

Therefore, meet the new kid on the blog when it comes to Microsoft Sentinel for SAP integration – SAP Ariba.

This cloud-native integration adds real-time threat detection, investigation, and response to your SAP Ariba environment and puts it into the context of your wider IT estate.

The bigger picture

Attackers use the easiest way in. Each month the SAP Security Patch Day starts a new race between hackers and defenders despite responsible disclosure obligations to allow a head start to defenders on reported vulnerabilities etc.

 

This race wears down defenses eventually – a gap is deemed to happen. Therefore, you need to be prepared to identify attackers in your IT landscape and be quick to lock them out again before they reach valuable targets.

Seeing the context and trail of the interconnected signals that the attacker leaves behind are key to identify compromise.

 

How It Works

  1. Create an application on your Ariba Developer portal to allow access to the audit-search api and collect your API key.
  2. Deploy the latest Microsoft Sentinel integration package in SAP Integration Suite. See this video for a similar scenario for guidance. Ignore the ERP specific settings on Cloud Connector etc. They don’t apply to Ariba.

3. Configure a Destination on SAP BTP for your Ariba instance and the audit search api.

 

Property

Value

Description

Name

Ariba-[TenantId]

Destination name (e.g., Ariba-p2pTeSap-2)

Type

HTTP

Connection type

URL

https://[region.]openapi.ariba.com/api/audit-search/v2/[prod or sandbox]

SAP Ariba Audit Search API URL (Find your base URl under Configuration Details)

Proxy Type

Internet

Always internet because of the cloud nature of the SAP service

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

For productive use

Client ID

[ClientId]

if applicable

Client Secret

[ClientSecret]

if applicable

Token Service URL

[TokenEndpoint]/v2/oauth/token

Ariba OAuth token endpoint

 

Additional Properties

Value

Description

tenantId

[TenantId]

SAP Ariba tenant ID

apiKey

[apiKey]

Api key for your SAP Ariba tenant

4. Deploy the agentless data connector for the Microsoft Sentinel Solution for SAP. See this video for a walk-through of the first steps and the official Microsoft Learn page here. Ignore the ERP specific settings on Cloud Connector etc. They don’t apply to Ariba

5. Connect your Ariba flow on the data connector pane to start ingesting SAP Ariba logs.

6. On the Advanced section supply the path “/community/SAPAribaAuditSearch” to point at the default route of the Ariba iFlow on SAP Integration Suite.

 

Observe the message flowing on Cloud Integration and Microsoft Sentinel. You can use the following query to verify the Ariba logs. Filter by AgentGuid in case you have multiple connections:

SAPAuditLog
| where AgentGuid == "Ariba"

 

Congratulations, you have successfully onboarded SAP Ariba to Sentinel for SAP 😎

 

There is one more thing!

Many of you are fronting Ariba with the SAP Cloud Identity Services. When you consult the attack graph from the beginning of this post, you already know that this is an important signal in the attack story. Identity compromise remains the number one attack path even in 2026. Have a look at the Digital Defense Report 2025 for more details.

Onboard your SAP Cloud Identity Service amongst your SAP BTP subaccounts to Sentinel for SAP from here to close that loop.

 

What you see is what you get

  1. AI enabled unified Security Operations
  • Correlate SAP Ariba events with enterprise telemetry in Microsoft Sentinel Solution for SAP and Microsoft Defender XDR ready for Microsoft Security Copilot.
  • Use prebuilt analytics rules, workbooks, and SOAR playbooks to detect and respond to threats like:
    • Privilege escalations
    • Unauthorized configuration changes
    • Suspicious transactions
  1. Compliance-Ready Log Retention
  • Store SAP logs cost-efficient in Microsoft Sentinel Data Lake for up to 12 years.
  • Support threat hunting involving SAP on the Sentinel Data lake through KQL jobs.

What’s Next

  • Enriching the mapping of the Ariba logs further to activate the remaining analytic rules provided by the SAP ERP private cloud offering.
  • Adding further Ariba specific detections. Which ones are top of mind for you? Reach out to me.

Final Words

That's a wrap 🎬 you saw today how simple SAP Ariba integration with your SIEM product can be. Remember: bringing SAP apps under the protection of your central SIEM isn't just a checkbox - it's essential for comprehensive security and compliance across your entire IT estate.

Quick link to GitHub.

Feel free to reach out to talk more SAP Ariba.

Cheers, Martin

entry.title: SAP Project Systems -BAPIs (A quick overview)
entry.content:
Core Project Management BAPIs

  • BAPI_PROJECT_MAINTAIN: The primary BAPI for creating and maintaining complete project structures including project definitions, WBS elements, networks, and activities.
  • BAPI_PROJECTDEF_CREATE: Create individual project definitions.
  • BAPI_PROJECTDEF_GETDETAIL: Retrieve detailed information about project definitions.
  • BAPI_PROJECTDEF_GETLIST: Obtain a list of project definitions based on selection criteria
  • BAPI_PROJECTDEF_EXISTENCECHECK: Check whether a specific project definition exists.
  • BAPI_PROJECT_GETINFO: Read detailed information about project definitions and their WBS elements.

WBS Element BAPIs

  • BAPI_BUS2054_GETDATA: Retrieve detailed information about WBS elements.
  • BAPI_BUS2054_CREATE_MULTI: Create multiple WBS elements in batch operations.
  • BAPI_BUS2054_CHANGE_MULTI: Modify multiple WBS elements simultaneously.
  • BAPI_BUS2054_DELETE_MULTI: Delete multiple WBS elements.
  • BAPI_BUS2054_GET_STATUS: Read active system and user statuses for WBS elements.

Network Activity BAPIs

  • BAPI_BUS2002_GETDATA: Get detailed data for network headers.
  • BAPI_BUS2002_CREATE: Create network headers.
  • BAPI_BUS2002_CHANGE: Modify network header information.
  • BAPI_BUS2002_ACT_CREATE_MULTI: Create multiple network activities.
  • BAPI_BUS2002_ACT_CHANGE_MULTI: Change multiple network activities.
  • BAPI_BUS2002_ACT_GETDATA: Retrieve detailed data for network activities.
  • BAPI_BUS2002_ACTELEM_CREATE_M: Create activity elements for networks.

Budget and Cost BAPIs

  • BAPI_COSTACTPLN_POSTPRIMCOST: Post primary costs for overhead planning.
  • BAPI_COSTACTPLN_READPRIMCOST: Read primary cost data from cost planning
  • BAPI_COSTACTPLN_POSTACTINPUT: Post activity input for cost planning.
  • BAPI_COSTACTPLN_READACTINPUT: Read activity input data

Resource Planning BAPIs

  • BAPI_ACTIVITYTYPE_GETDETAIL: Get detailed information about activity types.
  • BAPI_ACTIVITYTYPE_GETLIST: Retrieve list of activity types using selection criteria.
  • BAPI_ACTIVITYTYPE_GETPRICES: Determine activity prices for cost center/activity type combinations.
  • BAPI_ACTTYPE_CREATEMULTIPLE: Create multiple activity types.
  • BAPI_ACTTYPE_CHANGEMULTIPLE: Change multiple activity types.

Status Management BAPIs

  • BAPI_BUS2001_SET_STATUS: Set or revoke system and user statuses for project definitions.
  • BAPI_BUS2002_SET_STATUS: Set or reset network statuses.
  • BAPI_BUS2054_GET_STATUS: Read active system and user statuses for PSP elements.

BAPI Usage Examples

BAPI Name

Use Case

Input Parameters

Output

BAPI_PROJECT_MAINTAIN

Create complete project structure

Project definition data, WBS hierarchy, methods table

Project created with all components

BAPI_BUS2054_GETDATA

Extract WBS element details

WBS element key, language

Detailed WBS element information

BAPI_PROJECTDEF_GETLIST

Get project list for reporting

Company code, selection criteria

List of matching project definitions

BAPI_BUS2002_ACT_CREATE_MULTI

Batch create network activities

Network number, activity data table

Created activities with confirmation

Integration Considerations

  • Dependencies: PS BAPIs integrate with Controlling (CO), Materials Management (MM), and Financial Accounting (FI) modules.
  • Authorization: Requires appropriate PS authorization objects and transaction codes (CJ01, CJ02, CJ20N, etc.)
  • Performance: Use "Multi" BAPIs for batch operations to improve performance when processing multiple objects.
  • Initialization: PS BAPIs require calling BAPI_PS_INITIALIZATION before execution and BAPI_PS_PRECOMMIT before final commit.
  • Commit Handling: Use BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT after successful BAPI_PS_PRECOMMIT, or BAPI_TRANSACTION_ROLLBACK if errors occur

Related Function Modules

  • BAPI_PS_INITIALIZATION: Initialize PS BAPI processing environment.
  • BAPI_PS_PRECOMMIT: Validate all PS BAPI calls before final commit
  • Function modules in SE37: Search for "BAPI*" with business objects BUS2001 (Project Definition), BUS2054 (WBS Element), and BUS2002 (Network) for additional PS-related BAPIs.

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Public Cloud: Cross Company Code Purchasing O...
entry.content:
In S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, you can indeed use a cross‑company code purchasing organization for both Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfers (5HP) and Advanced Intercompany Sales (5D2) — there is no technical restriction in standard. These scope items are designed to support multi‑company scenarios.

Take into consideration the below points while going with the above approach

Master Data Consistency: Ensure vendor/customer assignments, intercompany pricing conditions, and tax codes are harmonized across all company codes.

Reporting: Since one purchasing org spans multiple companies, reporting at the purchasing org level will aggregate across them. Use company code/plant filters for granular reporting.

Authorizations: Carefully design roles so users don’t inadvertently gain access across companies.

Compliance: Intercompany tax and pricing must still be maintained per company code; the cross‑company purchasing org doesn’t override these requirements.

entry.title: Help With Transaction Notification
entry.content:
Hi All,

I have created two TN's on sales order which is working fine.

Working TN:


---- Prevent addition/ update of Sales Order if DocDueDate or ShipDate is Weekend or Holiday----

/* ROW LEVEL CHECK (ShipDate) */
IF EXISTS (
SELECT 1
FROM RDR1 T1
WHERE T1.DocEntry = @List_of_cols_val_tab_del
AND (
DATENAME(WEEKDAY, T1.ShipDate) IN ('Saturday','Sunday')
OR CAST(T1.ShipDate AS DATE) IN (SELECT CAST(StrDate AS DATE) FROM HLD1)
)
)
BEGIN
SET @error = 1717
SET @error_message = 'Sales Order line Ship Date cannot fall on a Weekend or Holiday.'
GOTO TN_RESULT
END


/* HEADER LEVEL CHECK (DocDueDate) */
IF EXISTS (
SELECT 1
FROM ORDR T0
WHERE T0.DocEntry = @List_of_cols_val_tab_del
AND (
DATENAME(WEEKDAY, T0.DocDueDate) IN ('Saturday','Sunday')
OR CAST(T0.DocDueDate AS DATE) IN (SELECT CAST(StrDate AS DATE) FROM HLD1)
)

)
BEGIN
SET @error = 1718
SET @error_message = 'Sales Order Due Date cannot fall on a Weekend or Holiday.'
GOTO TN_RESULT
END

This is working fine.

However, When I consolidate these into a single TN, it doesn't work.

Is this system behavior or something missing in the code:

----------------------------

Consolidated TN not working

--------------------------------

IF EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM ORDR T0
    INNER JOIN RDR1 T1 ON T0.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry
    WHERE T0.DocEntry = @List_of_cols_val_tab_del
      AND (
            -- Weekend/Holiday check for SO Due Date
            DATENAME(weekday, T0.DocDueDate) IN ('Saturday', 'Sunday')
            OR T0.DocDueDate IN (SELECT H.StrDate FROM HLD1 H)

            -- Weekend/Holiday check for Row ShipDate
            OR DATENAME(weekday, T1.ShipDate) IN ('Saturday', 'Sunday')
            OR T1.ShipDate IN (SELECT H.StrDate FROM HLD1 H)
          )
      AND T0.DocDueDate >= CAST(GETDATE() AS date)
)
BEGIN
    DECLARE @daytype NVARCHAR(100)

    SELECT TOP 1
        @daytype = CASE
                       WHEN DATENAME(weekday, T0.DocDueDate) IN ('Saturday','Sunday')
                            THEN 'Weekend (Header DocDueDate)'
                       WHEN DATENAME(weekday, T1.ShipDate) IN ('Saturday','Sunday')
                            THEN 'Weekend (Row ShipDate)'
                       WHEN T0.DocDueDate IN (SELECT StrDate FROM HLD1)
                            THEN 'Holiday (Header DocDueDate)'
                       WHEN T1.ShipDate IN (SELECT StrDate FROM HLD1)
                            THEN 'Holiday (Row ShipDate)'
                   END
    FROM ORDR T0
    INNER JOIN RDR1 T1 ON T0.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry
    WHERE T0.DocEntry = @List_of_cols_val_tab_del
      AND (
            DATENAME(weekday, T0.DocDueDate) IN ('Saturday', 'Sunday')
            OR DATENAME(weekday, T1.ShipDate) IN ('Saturday', 'Sunday')
            OR T0.DocDueDate IN (SELECT StrDate FROM HLD1)
            OR T1.ShipDate IN (SELECT StrDate FROM HLD1)
          )

    SET @error = 1717
    SET @error_message = 'Date cannot be on a ' + @daytype + '!'
    GOTO TN_RESULT
END

 

Can someone please check and let me know what is missing for the consolidated TN to make it work?

Question posted in Q&Z: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/help-with-transaction-notification/qaq-p/14324378

Regards,

Joseph

entry.title: Re: In SAP preventive maintenance work order basic start...
entry.content:
What you’re seeing is actually standard SAP scheduling logic in Plant Maintenance (PM) — the system is not “ignoring” your 7‑day factory calendar, but rather applying the rules of basic dates vs. scheduling dates.

  • Basic Dates (start/finish) are entered manually or derived from the maintenance plan.
  • Final Due Date comes from the maintenance plan cycle (e.g., monthly, yearly).
  • When you schedule, SAP checks the factory calendar assigned to the work center/plant.
  • Even if you’ve defined a 7‑day calendar, the system still respects the “normal scheduling logic”:
    • It calculates the start date backwards from the due date using the work center scheduling parameters (e.g., 5 working days lead time).
    • If the due date falls on a non‑working day (Saturday in your case), SAP shifts the date to the nearest valid working day.

Why it looks like 5 Days instead of 7 is because, the work center capacity category and scheduling parameters may still be set to a 5‑day week, even though the factory calendar is 7 days. In PM, both the factory calendar and the work center capacity settings must align. If the work center is defined with Monday–Friday as working days, the system will schedule accordingly, regardless of the factory calendar. That’s why your order is showing 31 Jan (Saturday) as “non‑working” and pushing the basic start date earlier.

To fix this, perform the following

  • Check Work Center Capacity Settings (CR02/CR03) and verify the capacity category and operating time. Ensure the work center is set to use the 7‑day calendar.
  • Check Maintenance Plan Scheduling Parameters and confirm the cycle and offset are aligned with 7‑day scheduling. Reschedule the Order after correcting the work center calendar assignment.

entry.title: Join Us Live: Ingesting SAP S/4HANA Master Data into SAP Customer Data Platform

In today's fast-paced business environment, efficiently managing and integrating customer data is key to improving operations and enhancing customer experiences. Our upcoming live session, "Ingesting SAP S/4HANA Master Data into SAP Customer Data Platform," is designed to help you navigate this critical aspect of data management.

Session Overview

Scheduled for March 19th, this 1.5-hour session will provide a practical demonstration of integrating SAP S/4HANA Master Data with SAP CX Customer Data Platform. Participants will gain a clear understanding of the steps involved in the full integration setup and its execution. The session aims to equip you with the knowledge needed to utilize customer data effectively in both B2B and B2C scenarios.

Key Objectives

  1. Understanding Integration Endpoints: Explore the integration endpoints of SAP S/4HANA and SAP Customer Data Platform. This foundational knowledge is crucial for establishing efficient connectivity between these platforms.

  2. Ingesting Sample Master Data: Learn how to ingest sample SAP S/4HANA Master Data into SAP CDP. The session will cover the technical aspects of data ingestion, offering practical insights.

  3. Generating Insights and Activating Data: Discover how to use ingested master data to generate actionable insights. We'll show you how to activate S/4HANA Master Data for effective B2B and B2C scenarios using Customer Data Profiles, Accounts, and Activities.

Who Should Attend?

This session is geared towards IT professionals, consultants, data engineers, and system integrators. If you're looking to improve your data strategy and understand the value of SAP integration, this session will provide valuable insights.

Join Us

entry.title: SAP University Alliances Excellence Award - Application Form >> Latin America & Caribbean 2026 <<
entry.content:
SAP Excellence Membership - Elevating Academic Partnership Through Recognition

The SAP Excellence Membership honors outstanding lighthouse institutions in the SAP University Alliances network. Recognition is awarded in three categories: 

1. SAP University Alliances Educational Excellence Membership (for outstanding curriculum contribution according to the SAP UA Portfolio Process, for outstanding achievements in SAP UA related competitions, for innovative ways to include SAP technology into academic teaching, innovative ways to utilize recent offerings like SAP Learning Hub, student edition, …) 
2. SAP University Alliances Research Excellence Membership (for outstanding research results and corresponding publications utilizing SAP technology / in the context of the SAP UA program, …)
3. SAP University Alliances Co-Innovation Excellence Membership (for outstanding engagement with the SAP ecosystem, e.g. via student projects on SAP Customer challenges, innovative POCs with SAP customers, SAP University Alliances
Lab activities, …) 

As a representative (lecturer) at an SAP University Alliances Member Institution, you can apply on behalf of your educational institution for one or more excellence membership awards by filling in this template and sending it to the global SAP University Alliances Inbox (universityalliances@sap.com) by March 31, 2026. 

👉For more details check out 📎 this guideline and in case of questions, do let us know!  

Best regards, 

Your SAP University Alliances team 

Latin America & the Caribbean

entry.title: Re: configuration missing and change showing only created...
entry.content: The error is because item category determination for LDPI is missing. In Public Cloud, you cannot add it yourself in CBC — it’s locked. You need to check scope item activation and, if still missing, raise an SAP incident so SAP can deliver the correct expert configuration.

entry.title: Paying with stablecoins in B2B scenarios - business process end-to-end
entry.content:
As an SAP S/4HANA user, you can pay your suppliers with stablecoins. Or receive payments in stablecoins. Key to it is SAP Digital Currency Hub. Here's a process-focused perspective on it.

Paying Supplier Invoices

Assuming your company is using SAP Digital Currency Hub, it's as easy to pay with stablecoins as paying the way you are used to: as an accounts payable accountant, you do a payment run. A dedicated payment method (you could use D for digital) would find all supplier invoices for business partners accepting stablecoins.

Payment Run in S/4HANA

Once the payment is released, it is sent via SAP's Multi-Bank Connector to the SAP Digital Currency Hub (DCH), which initiates payment immediately. It interacts with your custody provider, to process the stablecoin payment, and confirms execution of the payment, allowing to clear the payable in your accounts.

SAP Digital Currency Hub - Outgoing Payment

SAP Digital Currency Hub - Blockchain Transaction

Additionally, when doing the payment run, you should send a remittance advice from your ERP system, using the established ways like email or direct integrations, to the other party, detailing the payment's purpose.

Receiving Payment for Receivables

Assuming, you have communicated a dedicated destination address to your customer, they will direct their payment to it. SAP Digital Currency Hub communicates permanently with your custody provider to check for incoming payments on the blockchain networks. Once a payment is received, the address used tells, who has paid. Together with the payment date and amount, the customer's ID is reported back to your S/4HANA as an incoming payment.

As the currently used stablecoin format definitions (ERC-20) do not support fields like "note to payee" or "payment reference", a remittance advice, received on an established channel (see above), will help to reconcile this payment with an open receivable.

Destination addresses are a great way to receive payments. They can be seen as an alias for your wallet's address, generated only for a certain business partner. Hence, all payments received on this destination address can be attributed to them - hence only they know it.

Payment preparation in SAP Digital Currency Hub

As an accountant, you usually do not enter the SAP Digital Currency Hub, which is intended to run as a rather invisible component. Nevertheless, to establish the payment agreement for a business partner, you need to log in.

The payment agreement consists of a set of destination addresses.

  1. Receive-to addresses: For your customer, you will create a dedicated address they have to pay to. Payments received on this address will be attributed to this business partner. So, you need to communicate it to them.
  2. Pay-to addresses: As a customer, you will enter the destination address received from your supplier. You will direct all payments for this business partner to this address.

This allows to identify the payer based on the address he as paid to.

Optional: Replicating Receivables to SAP Digital Currency Hub

Receivables processing can be enhanced by replicating receivables to the hub. When an incoming payment drops in, it will reconcile the open receivable with these transactions. It applies a simple mechanism, determining the business partner by receive-to address (see above), and matching the receivable by amount, based on a FIFO principle. The benefit is enhanced note-to-payee information when the transaction is sent to the ERP solution, allowing instant clearing in accounts receivable.

Summary

Stablecoin transactions are fast, taking only seconds or minutes. They can be used to pay your suppliers, without modification of your ERP system. You only need to set-up a dedicated house bank account and a payment method, and to integrate with SAP Digital Currency Hub. Then, existing means like payment runs are used to trigger a payment, and bank statements received from the hub are used to receive payments. The hub itself does not need your attention, only at setup.

entry.title: Re: Issue with Performance Rating in Compensation
entry.content:
The configuration in the compensation form of the rating source have the following options other than default:  overallCompetency, overallObjective, OverallPerformance and overallPotential.

The default is the overallPerformance and that will give you the final rating as is configured in PM.   You also have the option to use the calculated rating and that should be set to No to ensure you get the manual rating vs. what is calculated.

Without seeing the actual configuration of the PM form and the Comp form it is impossible to give a definitive solution.   I would run a report against the PM form so you can see the values of all the ratings that are being produced in PM and that should help you with the mapping.

This should work but if it does not, you can store the rating outside of PM and map the pmRating to either an import key if not EC integrated or to an MDF if EC integrated.
